coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots to destroy bacteria competing with Campylobacter jejuni in enrichment medium, and development of a monoclonal antibody to detect C. jejuni after enrichment
coredata.dc:creator: Ha, Jimyeong
Seo, Yeongeun
Kim, Yujin
Lee, Jeeyeon
Lee, Heeyoung
Kim, Sejeong
Choi, Yukyung
Oh, Hyemin
Lee, Yewon
Park, Eunyoung
Kang, Joohyun
Yoon, Yohan
coredata.dc:description: The objective of this study was to develop a method with improved sensitivity for Campylobacter jejuni detection in foods. Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) were synthesized and added to an enrichment medium (Bolton broth) at a concentration of 10 mg/mL. A light-emitting diode (LED) at a wavelength of 425 nm was used to irradiate the N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium to induce an exothermic reaction for 1 h. Additionally, a monoclonal antibody specific to C. jejuni NCTC11168 was developed using hybridoma cells to aid detection. The C. jejuni detection capabilities of N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium and the conventional Bolton broth enrichment, were compared using duck samples. C. jejuni in the enrichment was detected with the monoclonal antibody based-indirect enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ID-ELISA). The N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium showed a better C. jejuni detection capability than the conventional Bolton broth enrichment. Additionally, data from ID-ELISA showed excellent detection efficiency and a shortened detection time in the N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium after LED irradiation at 425 nm. These results indicate that 1-h LED irradiation at 425 nm to Bolton broth supplemented with the N-CNDs increased the detection efficiency and shortened the detection time with the monoclonal antibody for C. jejuni in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Campylobacter jejuni
Detection method
Nanodots
Monoclonal antibody
Light emitting diode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Examination of various light-emitting diode wavelengths for identifying antibacterial activity
Examination of the antibacterial effect of N-CNDs
Employment of the developed detection method for bacterial detection in food
Determination of the LED wavelength and N-CNDs concentration that exert antibacterial activity
Detection of C. jejuni in duck samples using the developed method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Ha J, and Yoon Y participated in the design of the study, analyzed the data, and wrote the manuscript. Lee J, Lee H, and Kim S helped draft the manuscript. Seo Y, Kim Y, Choi Y, Oh H, and Lee Y performed the experiments. Kang J, and Park E performed the experiments and coordination of the study. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Changes in Campylobacter jejuni cell counts during LED irradiation at wavelengths of 425, 565, and 700 nm for 300 min.
Changes in the counts of Campylobacter jejuni in Bolton broth and Bolton broth supplemented with N-CNDs after LED irradiation at a wavelength of 425 nm for 300 min.
Campylobacter jejuni cell counts at different concentrations of N-CNDs after 1 h LED irradiation at 425 nm; *, significantly different (p < 0.05).
Acinetobacter baumannii cell counts at different concentrations of N-CNDs after 1 h LED irradiation at 425 nm; *, significantly different (p < 0.05).
Acinetobacter baumannii (A), Staphylococcus aureus (B), and Listeria monocytogenes (C) cell counts in Bolton broth and Bolton broth supplemented with N-CNDs while irradiating the 425 nm LED for 1 h.

Campylobacter jejuni (A) and Acinetobacter baumannii (B) cell counts in Bolton broth and Bolton broth supplemented with N-CNDs during enrichment at 24 h and 48 h; *: significantly different (p < 0.05).

Specificity of the monoclonal antibody, measured by indirect ID-ELISA; AB, Acinetobacter baumannii; EC, Escherichia coli; PA, Pseudomonas aeruginosa; PF, Pseudomonas fluorescence; SE, Salmonella enterica; ST, Salmonella typhimurium; LM, Listeria monocytogenes; CC, Campylobacter coli; CJ, Campylobacter jejuni.
Determination of optimal monoclonal antibody dilution times using ID-ELISA.

Qualitative analysis of Campylobacter jejuni in duck carcasses using ID-ELISA after enrichment in 2× Bolton broth (2 × B), concentrated suspension of enrichment in 2× Bolton broth (C2 × B), and 2× Bolton broth supplemented with N-CNDs (2 × B with N-CNDs).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots to destroy bacteria competing with Campylobacter jejuni in enrichment medium, and development of a monoclonal antibody to detect C. jejuni after enrichment

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Food and Nutrition, Sookmyung Women's University, Seoul 04310, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The objective of this study was to develop a method with improved sensitivity for Campylobacter jejuni detection in foods. Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) were synthesized and added to an enrichment medium (Bolton broth) at a concentration of 10 mg/mL. A light-emitting diode (LED) at a wavelength of 425 nm was used to irradiate the N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium to induce an exothermic reaction for 1 h. Additionally, a monoclonal antibody specific to C. jejuni NCTC11168 was developed using hybridoma cells to aid detection. The C. jejuni detection capabilities of N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium and the conventional Bolton broth enrichment, were compared using duck samples. C. jejuni in the enrichment was detected with the monoclonal antibody based-indirect enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ID-ELISA). The N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium showed a better C. jejuni detection capability than the conventional Bolton broth enrichment. Additionally, data from ID-ELISA showed excellent detection efficiency and a shortened detection time in the N-CNDs-supplemented enrichment medium after LED irradiation at 425 nm. These results indicate that 1-h LED irradiation at 425 nm to Bolton broth supplemented with the N-CNDs increased the detection efficiency and shortened the detection time with the monoclonal antibody for C. jejuni in food.

ce:section.ce:para: Campylobacter jejuni is a major bacterium responsible for causing foodborne illness worldwide through poultry (Friedman et al., 2000; Hansson et al., 2018). However, the number of outbreaks may be underestimated because of the difficulties in detection caused by competing bacteria such as Acinetobacter baumannii and Escherichia coli (Kordowska-Wiater and Stasiak, 2011; Lee et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019). Thus, it is important to develop an accurate and sensitive detection method for C. jejuni.

In C. jejuni selective media, antibiotics, especially β-lactam antibiotics such as cephalosporin and carbapenem, are added to inhibit the growth of other bacteria (Chon et al., 2017; Jo et al., 2017; Yoo et al., 2014). The International Organization for Standardization (ISO) recommends to use Bolton broth and modified charcoal cefoperazone deoxycholate agar (mCCDA) to detect Campylobacter in food (ISO, 2006), which contain the antibiotics to control the growth of other bacterial competitors and the ingredients for growth. However, because of the indiscriminate use of antibiotics (Rana et al., 2019), there has been an increase in bacteria expressing extended-spectrum beta-lactamases (ESBL), which are resistant to antibiotics (Cetin et al., 2020; Coque et al., 2008). Therefore, alternatives to antibiotics needs to be developed to improve the efficiency of C. jejuni detection.

Various materials have been developed as alternatives for antibiotics, among which bactericidal nanomaterials and techniques are being rapidly developed (Meziani et al., 2016). Among these, carbon nanodots, which cause the photothermal reaction using light as a catalyst, can be excellent candidates for antibacterial action. They are more stable, and biocompatible, and have higher water solubility, and better photostability than gold or silver nanoparticles (Feng et al., 2016; Hong et al., 2015). Hence, it is necessary to study if carbon nanodots can be antibiotic substitutes in the Campylobacter enrichment broth. Several new culture media have been developed for detecting C. jejuni (Chon et al., 2017; Jo et al., 2017; Yoo et al., 2014), but these methods require long culture times and incubation under microaerobic conditions (Brandl et al., 2004). Recently, more rapid detection techniques, such as nucleic acid-based methods, biosensor-based methods, and immunology-based methods, have been developed, but they have high detection limits or involve long pretreatment processes (Ivnitski et al., 1999; Mandal et al., 2011; Park et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2014). If C. jejuni can be selectively enriched by destroying the competing bacteria, the detection limit and time can be improved.

Therefore, the objective of this study was to develop a selective medium that destroys competing bacteria, but not of C. jejuni, thereby enriching the bacterium, and a monoclonal antibody to rapidly detect C. jejuni after enrichment, which may result in improved detection sensitivity, limit, and time.

C. jejuni NCTC11168 (National Collection of Type Cultures) was stored at −70 °C in a bead stock (AES Chemunex, Combourg, France). The bead stock was streaked on Columbia agar (BioMérieux, Marcy-l'Étoile, France) and incubated at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions (CO2: 2.5–9.5%, O2: 6.2–13.2%), using a CampyGen gas generating system (Campygen; Oxoid Ltd., Basingstoke, UK). Colonies on the plates were resuspended in 5 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4; 0.2 g of KH2PO4; 1.5 g of Na2HPO4.7H2O; 8.0 g of NaCl; and 0.2 g of KCl in 1 L of distilled water) using a loop. The suspensions were centrifuged at 1912 ×g at 4 °C for 15 min and washed twice with PBS. The supernatants were then discarded. The cell pellets were resuspended in PBS and serially diluted with PBS to achieve 5 log CFU/mL. Five Staphylococcus aureus strains ATCC10826, ATCC13565, ATCC14458, ATCC23235, and ATCC27664 (American Type Culture Collection), and four Escherichia coli strains NCCP11142, NCCP14038, NCCP14039, and NCCP15661 (National Culture Collection for Pathogens) were stored at −70 °C in 20% glycerol stock. They were inoculated in 10 mL tryptic soy broth (TSB; Becton, Dickinson and Company, Sparks, MD, USA) and incubated at 35 °C for 24 h. An aliquot (0.1 mL) of the culture was transferred into 10 mL of fresh TSB and subcultured at 35 °C for 24 h. The subcultures of S. aureus and E. coli were centrifuged and washed with PBS as described above. The suspensions were then diluted with PBS to achieve 5 log CFU/mL. Three Listeria monocytogenes strains NCCP10806, NCCP10807, and NCCP10809 were stored at −70 °C in 20% glycerol stock. They were inoculated in 10 mL TSB with yeast extract (TSBYE; BD) and incubated at 30 °C for 24 h. An aliquot (0.1 mL) of the culture was transferred into 10 mL of fresh TSBYE and subcultured at 30 °C for 24 h. The subcultures of L. monocytogenes were centrifuged and washed with PBS as described above. The suspension was then diluted with PBS to achieve 5 log CFU/mL. Acinetobacter baumannii KACC12454 (Korean Agricultural Culture Collection) was stored at −70 °C in 20% glycerol stock. It was inoculated in 10 mL Luria-Bertani broth (LB; BD) and incubated at 35 °C for 24 h. An aliquot (0.1 mL) of the culture was transferred into 10 mL of fresh LB broth and subcultured at 35 °C for 24 h. The subcultures of A. baumannii were centrifuged and washed with PBS as described above. The suspension was then diluted with PBS to achieve 5 log CFU/mL.

Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) were synthesized using a method modified from the one described by Lee et al. (2016). Citric acid (1 g) (Sigma-Aldrich) was dissolved in 1 mL sterile distilled water. Oleylamine (1 mL) (Sigma-Aldrich) and octadecene (9 mL) (Sigma-Aldrich) were added to a round-bottom flask and the prepared citric acid solution was added to the round-bottom flask. After vigorously stirring this mixture, a 7 M solution of nitric acid (Sigma-Aldrich) was added, and the mixture was heated at 250 °C for 3 h. The solution (5 mL) was added to methanol (40 mL) (Sigma-Aldrich) and centrifuged three times at 1977 ×g for 15 min. The pellets were dried in a vacuum oven at 65 °C with −0.07 MPa overnight. The resulting solution (100 mg) was then dissolved in toluene (10 mL) and mixed with ethanolamine (20 mL). It was then heated at 100 °C for 30 min under stirring and transferred to a vacuum evaporator for solidification. The final solid was dissolved in distilled water (20 mL) and dialyzed three times using Spectra/Por Biotech Cellulose Ester dialysis tubes (100–500 Da). After dialysis, the liquid was collected and freeze-dried overnight. The particles were blocked from light and stored in a freezer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of various light-emitting diode wavelengths for identifying antibacterial activity
ce:section.ce:para: Light-emitting diodes (LEDs; SIGMA LED CO., LTD, Incheon, South Korea) emitting wavelengths of 425 nm, 565 nm, and 700 nm were used to irradiate C. jejuni NCTC11168, S. aureus, and L. monocytogenes in each enrichment medium for 30, 60, and 300 min, respectively, to select the wavelengths with antimicrobial activities. After irradiation, cells were plated on modified charcoal cefoperazone deoxycholate agar (mCCDA; Oxoid), mannitol salt agar (MSA; BD), and Palcam agar (BD) to calculate the survival of C. jejuni, S. aureus, and L. monocytogenes, respectively.

The synthesized N-CNDs were added to 4 mL Bolton broth (Oxoid) at 20 mg/mL, and the C. jejuni NCTC11168 were inoculated in Bolton broth with N-CNDs at 5 log CFU/mL. For comparison, treatment groups were divided into the following: i) no N-CNDs added and no LED irradiation, ii) no N-CNDs added and 5 h LED irradiation at 425 nm; iii) N-CNDs added and no LED irradiation; iv) N-CNDs added and 5 h LED irradiation at 425 nm. Each sample was plated on mCCDA at 30 min, 60 min, and 300 min to estimate C. jejuni NCTC11168 survival. The mCCDA plates were incubated at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions. The colonies were manually counted to determine the optimal time for LED irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examination of the antibacterial effect of N-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized N-CNDs were added to 2 mL Bolton broth (Oxoid) at 0.1, 1, 5, 10, and 20 mg/mL, and C. jejuni NCTC11168 or A. baumannii were inoculated in Bolton broth with N-CNDs at 5 log CFU/mL. The samples were then irradiated with the LED at the selected wavelength for 1 h, and then plated on mCCDA to enumerate C. jejuni NCTC11168 survival or on Luria-Bertani agar (LB agar; BD) to determine A. baumannii survival. Also, A. baumannii, S. aureus, and L. monocytogenes were inoculated in Bolton broth with N-CNDs. After that, the samples were irradiated with the LED at the selected wavelength for 1 h, and plated on LB agar to determine A. baumannii survival, on MSA to determine S. aureus survival, and on Palcam agar to determine L. monocytogenes survival. The mCCDA plates were incubated at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions, and LB agar (35°C), MSA (35°C), and Palcam agar (35°C) were incubated for 24 h under aerobic conditions. The colonies were manually counted to determine the optimal concentration of N-CNDs.

To confirm the usability of the enrichment media for C. jejuni detection; a competing bacterium (A. baumannii) and C. jejuni NCTC11168 were inoculated into Bolton broth and Bolton broth supplemented with 10 mg/mL N-CNDs. All inoculated media were then irradiated with LED at 425 nm for 1 h in a dark box (prepared in-house) and cultured at 42 °C for 48 h. The cultures were plated on mCCDA or LB agar after 24 h and 48 h. The plates were incubated at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions for C. jejuni and incubated at 35 °C for 24 h under aerobic conditions for A. baumannii. The colonies on the plates were then manually counted.

C. jejuni NCTC11168 resuspended in PBS were incubated in a water bath at 98 °C for 20 min to inactivate the bacterial cells, and then brought to room temperature to cool down. To prepare the antigen, the 8 log CFU/mL C. jejuni NCTC11168 suspension was mixed with complete or incomplete Freund's adjuvant (Sigma-Aldrich) at 1:1 (v/v), and emulsified for 30 min, using an immune syringe (Becton, Dickinson and Company) connected to a 3-way stopcock (Becton, Dickinson and Company). The emulsified antigen (200 μL) was injected into the abdominal cavity of a five-week old female BALB/c mice. This immunization was performed four times, once every two weeks. At the last immunization, only 200 μL of the dead bacterial suspension was injected to boost antibody production. All animal protocols were approved by the Institute of Animal Care and Use Committee in Sookmyung Women's University (Approval number: SMWU-IACUC-1612-042). For fusion, myeloma cells (Sp2/0-Ag14 cells, KCLB 21581) purchased from the Korean Cell Line Bank (KCLB, Seoul, Korea) were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM; HyClone™, South Logan, Utah, USA) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS; HyClone™) and gentamicin (Sigma-Aldrich). Mice that showed high antibody production capacity were euthanized to obtain spleen cells. Red blood cell lysis buffer (Sigma-Aldrich) was mixed with the isolated spleen cells and myeloma cells. PEG1500 (Roche, IN, USA) was then added to the cell mixture followed by manual shaking at 37 °C. The suspension was centrifuged at 135 ×g for 5 min at 25 °C, and the supernatant was discarded. The cell pellet was resuspended in cell culture medium (DMEM + FBS + gentamicin) using a pipette. The suspension (50 μL) was dispensed into 96-well plates. On days 1, 3, 5, and 7, the medium containing hypoxanthine-aminopterin-thymidine (HAT; Sigma-Aldrich) was added to the wells, and when the cells were attached by 1/3 of the well surface, the antibody producing ability of the cells and cross-reactivity with other bacteria were measured using indirect enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ID-ELISA). Cells that had high antibody production ability were transferred to a 24-well plate (SPL LIFE SCIENCE, Pocheon-si, Korea) and clones were allowed to propagate, and the culture of one cell was confirmed. The cell clones were injected into the abdominal cavity of mice at a dose of 107 cells/200 μL per animal. The ascites fluid formed in response was collected using a syringe, and purified using the ammonium sulfate precipitation method (Grodzki and Berenstein, 2010). The purified antibody was lyophilized and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Employment of the developed detection method for bacterial detection in food
ce:section.ce:para: Seven duck carcasses were purchased from a local market. They were placed into zipper bags containing 800 mL of 0.1% buffered peptone water (BPW; BD). The zipper bags were shaken manually for 1 min, and 20 mL aliquots of the rinsates were transferred to 20-mL 2× Bolton broth (2 × B) (MFDS, 2020) and 20-mL 2 × B containing N-CNDs (2 × B with N-CNDs) at 10 mg/mL. In addition, the 10-fold concentrated rinsates were transferred to 20-mL 2 × B (C2 × B) (Ryu et al., 2010). The 2 × B with 10 mg/mL N-CNDs were irradiated with LED at 425 nm for 1 h before incubation. All enrichment media 2 × B, 2 × B with N-CNDs and C2 × B were incubated microaerobically at 42 °C for 48 h. The enrichments were centrifuged at 4942 ×g and 25 °C for 5 min, and the pellets were washed twice with PBS. The suspensions in PBS were subjected to an ID-ELISA using the developed antibody.

This experiment was repeated 3 times (n = 3). All data were analyzed using the general linear model procedure of SAS® (version 9.3; SAS Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). LS means among the fixed effects were compared using a pairwise t-test at α = 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the LED wavelength and N-CNDs concentration that exert antibacterial activity
Detection of C. jejuni in duck samples using the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: Duck carcass was chosen to evaluate the efficiency of the developed method in the food matrix, because C. jejuni is mostly detected in raw poultry rather than any other food matrix. Duck samples were enriched in 2 × B, C2 × B, and 2 × B with N-CNDs after LED irradiation at 425 nm. The enriched media were centrifuged, and the pellet was suspended in PBS. ID-ELISA was then performed with the developed monoclonal antibody using the suspension. The results showed that the detection rate was much higher in the culture media supplemented with N-CNDs than in 2 × B (Table 1 ). N-CNDs were thought to destroy background flora, but not C. jejuni, by exothermic reaction during LED irradiation at 425 nm for 1 h, which allowed for better enrichment of C. jejuni, resulting in a high detection rate in ID-ELISA. The conventional method for detecting C. jejuni in food involves culturing in pre-enrichment media for 2 days and streaking on selective agar, followed by incubation for 2 days. Thus, this method is laborious and time-consuming. However, the method developed in this study is more effective at improving the C. jejuni detection efficiency and is less time-intensive than the conventional method.

In conclusion, the addition of N-CNDs and the LED irradiation of 425 nm inhibited the growth of A. baumannii, which is often detected together with C. jejuni. Therefore, the use of the 2 × B containing N-CNDs at 10 mg/mL, followed by 1 h LED irradiation at 425 nm prior to enrichment, improved the detection efficiency of C. jejuni, and the use of the monoclonal antibody developed in this study further reduced the detection time. Regarding the detection by the monoclonal antibody, it may be limited to some strains of C. jejuni because the monoclonal antibody was verified only with C. jejuni NCTC11168. Thus, it should be verified with other stains of C. jejuni in the future. In addition, since only duck samples were examined with the monoclonal antibody, other foods needs to be verified with the antibody.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
No competing financial interests exist.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Ha J, and Yoon Y participated in the design of the study, analyzed the data, and wrote the manuscript. Lee J, Lee H, and Kim S helped draft the manuscript. Seo Y, Kim Y, Choi Y, Oh H, and Lee Y performed the experiments. Kang J, and Park E performed the experiments and coordination of the study. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.
